headline: Toby is motivated but growth is blocked by post-promotion caution: he avoids political risk, lacks sponsorship clarity, and needs decision ownership to believe in the next level.
summary_bullets: Toby links the promotion outcome to changed behavior: “more careful about where I spend political capital,” which is now shaping where he shows up.
His growth gap is not execution—it's translation to org influence: “positive feedback on craft and collaboration” but “less clarity on what separates me at the next level.”
He wants a contract with proof and authority, not retrospectives: “milestones that show progress before the next cycle” and “one leadership bet where I drive the decision.”
understanding_paragraph: We learned Toby’s main blocker is confidence + sponsorship mechanics, not effort: he avoids high-ambiguity forums because he doesn’t want to look underprepared. The session clarified that “next level” for him means cross-org decision ownership plus visible strategic influence, not just strong design outcomes. We also learned he will commit only when the growth plan is framed as two concrete leadership bets with forward milestones.

axes.meaning: No clear wellbeing signal; the conversation focused on political risk, sponsorship, and decision ownership.
Weak-to-moderate engagement read: he stays motivated but is holding back in “high-ambiguity forums” to avoid political exposure.
Clarity is slightly low: he wants clearer separation criteria for “the next level” and clearer sponsorship in priority-setting rooms.
Strong negative growth signal: he needs a decision-ownership contract with milestones; without it, he stalls at influence and forum participation.

brutal_truth_employee: Toby is not blocked by motivation; he said, “I am still motivated, but the promotion outcome made me more careful about where I spend political capital.” He also admits the core gap: “less clarity on what separates me at the next level,” and he avoids risk because “I do not want to sound underprepared.”
brutal_truth_manager: In this 1:1, Toby gave a clear growth contract requirement: “If we define two concrete leadership bets for this half, I can commit fully.” The moment to lock it in is when he asked for “milestones that show progress before the next cycle, not only retrospective feedback”—the next step is to convert that into specific ownership and a concrete decision path with a named stakeholder sponsorship plan.

next_actions.action: Draft Toby’s growth contract with two named leadership bets, each with an explicit cross-org decision he will drive and a measurable forward milestone by end of this half.
Schedule a 30-minute stakeholder alignment with the priority-setting decision-makers for Toby’s chosen decision area and secure written sponsorship/air-cover for the rooms he must attend.
Run the next 1:1 as a decision-ownership review: ask Toby what decision he drove, what tradeoff he influenced, and what sponsorship milestone was or wasn’t delivered.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Toby continues avoiding high-ambiguity forums, treat it as the political-capital caution staying active, not as normal nerves.
Before the next cycle: if Toby only reports retrospective feedback (not forward milestones), the growth contract is not creating the proof he requested.
